input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, where the first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor. This industrial robot operates in a high-precision manufacturing environment requiring dynamic motion control, real-time sensor feedback, and precise trajectory tracking. Its complex mechanical dynamics, multi-axis coordination requirements, and susceptibility to joint friction make it ideal for digital twinning to enhance operational accuracy and reliability.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for multi-level abstraction modeling (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer); architecture description languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector perspectives; simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink; general-purpose programming languages C++ and Java; UML class diagrams for the Digital Shadow Reference Model; and methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to manage system complexity.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system utilizes real-time data-driven representations with live measurements and time series of all sensors for synchronization, maintaining temporal granularity based on sensor sampling rates to ensure accurate state representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture follows a Digital Shadow Reference Model defined via UML class diagram, comprising components such as Purpose, Data Trace, Asset, Processing, and Model, integrated using component & connector perspective with ADLs including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for structural and behavioral description.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including SysML for structural (geometric) modeling via Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs), MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines for behavioral modeling, and MathWorks Simulink for analytical simulations. Data originates from physical system states through one-way data flow as defined in the digital shadow reference model, managed via Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as AADL, ACME, and MontiArc, integrated with simulation tools (Simulink), general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), and abstraction levels (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers) under methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT to address heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data, including input current and output angle, are transmitted from the physical system to the digital twin via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for data transmission between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs a structured methodology comprising Problem identification, Asset and model analysis, Use or creation of calculations or models, and Data identification steps, as outlined in Sections II.C and IV.A of the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction to improve robot motion accuracy, with user interaction via a unified dashboard for real-time data visualization and decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML [17] for systems engineering modeling, ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25, 26] for architecture description languages, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) [17, 27] for component and connector perspectives. Interoperability between heterogeneous modeling tools and notations is addressed through standardized interfaces to integrate stakeholder perspectives [31].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the MABI MAX 100 robot, its six-axis configuration, and the specific motor type for the first axis. It correctly contextualizes the system within high-precision manufacturing (machining) and highlights the issues with friction and accuracy that motivate the digital twinning. Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', and 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text focuses on the construction and methodology of a 'digital shadow' for data integration and friction modeling. While the Abstract mentions the goal is to 'improve robot motion accuracy' and Section IV mentions 'visualization in a dashboard', the text does not explicitly define a specific list of 'Digital Twin services' (such as monitoring, optimization, prediction) as a distinct characteristic of the system. It distinguishes between the passive digital shadow and the active digital twin but does not detail the services. Therefore, the statement that it is not in the document is correct.

The extraction is highly detailed and perfectly supported by Section II.A of the document. It lists the specific tools and methodologies mentioned: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) with SysML', 'architecture description languages (ADLs) including ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs', 'simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink', 'general-purpose programming languages C++ and Java', and 'methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT'. It also correctly notes the use of 'UML class diagrams for the Digital Shadow Reference Model' in Section II.B.

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],

and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe

ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML

Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s

or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from

shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description correctly identifies the real-time, data-driven nature of the system. The source text mentions 'real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior' and 'live measurements' in the Introduction. It also notes in Section IV.A.4 that 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT,' supporting the synchronization aspect. However, the text does not explicitly detail specific sampling rates or latency requirements, only implying them through the 'live' and 'real-time' descriptors.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on a single digital shadow instance ('one digital shadow') for a specific robot axis and does not discuss multiple instances, hierarchical structures of twins, or coordination between separate twins.

The description accurately summarizes the architectural components and modeling languages found in the text. Section II.B presents a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model' as a 'UML class diagram' comprising 'Purpose, Data Trace, Data Point, Metadata, Asset, and Model.' Section II.A lists 'ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs' as ADLs used for component and connector perspectives. The extraction correctly synthesizes these details regarding the system's orchestration.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' in the abstract and 'MQTT' for sensor streaming, it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards implementation details, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins. The focus remains on the internal structure and the physical asset connection.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document mentions the 'development phase' regarding modeling languages (Section II.A) and 'operation' of the robot, but it does not describe the lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin itself, nor does it cover transition management or lifecycle governance approaches as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The description accurately matches the structured methodology defined in the text. Section II.C explicitly lists the steps: '1) Problem identification', '2) Asset and model analysis', '3) Use or creation of calculations or models', and '4) Data identification'. Section IV.A confirms that this method is applied 'following each step'.

The text states that the resulting digital shadows enable the 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction' to 'improve robot motion accuracy'. It also specifies user interaction via 'visualization in a dashboard' (Abstract) and 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard' (Introduction) to support understanding of physical relationships.

The extraction accurately identifies the standards mentioned in Section II.A, including 'SysML [17] for systems engineering modeling' and 'ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26]' as architecture description languages. It also correctly cites the integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives [31] related to interoperability.
